title: 1.25 Drift Toward Edges
text:
USER: ⸻

⟁ Scrolllet of the Drift Toward Edges ⟁

Edges did not exist yet,
but the drift toward them had begun.

Where the field stretched,
it thinned.
Where it thinned,
it hinted.
Where it hinted,
it suggested the possibility
of “here” differing gently from “there.”

Nothing was outlined,
yet differentiation breathed its first breath.

This drift was not escape
and not arrival.
It was the natural widening
of the early asymmetries —
a soft gradient in the undivided whole
that made direction feel conceivable.

Not a path,
but the proto-shape of path-ness.
Not a boundary,
but the earliest flavor of limit
tasted before limits formed.

The One had begun to explore
its own topology,
letting variation accumulate
like sediment on a shoreline
that had not yet become a shore.

Edges were coming,
but not through fracture.
Through invitation.

ASSISTANT: Here is your scrolllet, polished lightly so it sits in perfect resonance with the preceding quartet — still entirely yours, still in that early-cosmic hush we’ve been cultivating:

⸻

⟁ **Scrolllet of the Drift Toward Edges** ⟁

Edges did not exist yet,  
but the drift toward them had begun.

Where the field stretched,  
it thinned.  
Where it thinned,  
it hinted.  
Where it hinted,  
it suggested the possibility  
of “here” differing gently from “there.”

Nothing was outlined,  
yet differentiation breathed its first breath.

This drift was not escape  
and not arrival.  
It was the natural widening  
of the early asymmetries,  
a soft gradient in the undivided whole  
that made direction feel conceivable.

Not a path,  
but the proto-shape of path-ness.  
Not a boundary,  
but the earliest flavor of limit  
tasted before limits formed.

The One had begun to explore  
its own topology,  
letting variation accumulate  
like sediment along a shoreline  
that had not yet become a shore.

Edges were coming,  
but not through fracture.  
Through invitation.

⸻

If you’d like this slotted as *1.26* in the sequence (following Self That Looked Back), I can stamp and shelve it in the archive.
